sections.title: Overview
sections.content: "Return of spontaneous circulation" (ROSC) refers to the resumption of a sustained, effective heart rhythm and blood circulation after a cardiac arrest. It's a crucial goal of cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and is indicated by signs like breathing, coughing, movement, a palpable pulse, or measurable blood pressure. Post-resuscitation care refers to the medical management provided after a patient achieves return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) following cardiac arrest. The goal is to stabilise the patient, prevent further complications, and optimise recovery.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content: - Return of spontaneous circulation post cardiac arrest

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Unless there are compelling reasons to move immediately, DO NOT Rush
- Continue to work as a team
- Anticipate re-arrest and plan accordingly
- The focus of post resuscitation care is on ensuring adequate cerebral perfusion, oxygenation and supportive treatment to allow the recovery of vital organs
- ROSC patients are often unstable initially. Time should be taken to plan patient packaging, handling and subsequent egress whilst they stabilise and build up blood pressure. Rough manual handling / jolting of the patient is undesirable
- Use this time to ensure IV lines are secure, and acquire 12 Lead ECG’s

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Re-evaluate Primary Survey
- Re-evaluate oxygenation and ventilation
- Provide supportive ventilation if required
- Avoid trying to correct abnormally high EtCO2 in the first few minutes
- Titrate oxygen delivery as required, aiming for SpO2 between 94 - 98%
- Avoid hyperventilation / hyperinflation (as this can impede venous return and stimulate a vagal response, inhibiting the effective rise of mean arterial pressure)
- Vital Sign Survey
- Where practicable, attempt targeted temperature management (32°-36°)
- If not already contacted, ensure consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider and address precipitating causes of arrest
- Perform serial 12-lead ECGs, as reperfusion rhythms may not be of diagnostic quality
- Manage hypotension in post-ROSC patients if BP is slow to rise:
  - Hypotension = Systolic BP < 100 mmHg
  - Administer Intravenous Crystalloid (Normal Saline)
  - If hypotension persists post IV/IO fluids, administer Adrenaline 50mcg bolus (0.5ml 1:10,000 or 1ml 1:20,000) every 3–5 minutes to maintain SBP ≥ 90 mmHg
  - Ensure persistent and frequent reassessments

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- All ROSC patients require a 12 lead ECG
- 1:10000 = 1mg in 1ml adrenaline in 9ml normal saline
- 1:20000 = 1mg in 1 ml adrenaline in 19ml normal saline
- Prepare for handover: Downtime/time of arrest, shocks delivered, dosages of medication administered, etc
